content:
# James Chapter 2 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 263 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/JAS_002_page_001.jpg

---

1. My brothers, do not hold the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory, with partiality.

2. For if a man wearing a gold ring and fine clothing comes into your assembly, and a poor man in filthy clothing also comes in,

3. and you look upon the one wearing the fine clothing and say, "You sit here in a good place," and to the poor man you say, "You stand there," or "Sit below my footstool,"

4. have you not made distinctions among yourselves and become judges with evil reasonings?

5. Listen, my beloved brothers: did not God choose the poor of the world to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom which he promised to those who love him?

6. But you have dishonored the poor man. Do not the rich oppress you, and do they not themselves drag you into courts?

7. Do they not blaspheme the good name that was invoked over you?

8. If indeed you fulfill the royal law according to the Scripture, "You shall love your neighbor as yourself," you do well.

9. But if you show partiality, you commit sin, being convicted by the law as transgressors.

10. For whoever keeps the whole law but stumbles in one point has become guilty of all.

11. For he who said, "Do not commit adultery," also said, "Do not murder." Now if you do not commit adultery but you do murder, you have become a transgressor of the law.

12. So speak and so act as those who are to be judged by the law of liberty.

13. For judgment is without mercy to the one who has shown no mercy. Mercy triumphs over judgment.

14. What is the profit, my brothers, if someone says he has faith but does not have works? Is the faith able to save him?

15. If a brother or sister is naked and lacking daily food,

16. and one of you says to them, "Go in peace, be warmed and be filled," but you do not give them the things needful for the body, what is the profit?

17. So also faith, if it does not have works, is dead by itself.

18. But someone will say, "You have faith, and I have works." Show me your faith apart from the works, and I will show you faith from my works.

19. You believe that God is one. You do well; even the demons believe, and shudder.

20. But do you wish to know, O empty man, that faith apart from works is idle?

21. Was not Abraham our father justified by works, when he offered up Isaac his son upon the altar?

22. You see that faith was working together with his works, and by the works the faith was made complete.

23. And the Scripture was fulfilled that says, "Abraham believed God, and it was reckoned to him as righteousness," and he was called a friend of God.

24. You see that a man is justified by works, and not by faith alone.

25. And likewise was not also Rahab the harlot justified by works, when she received the messengers and sent them out by another way?

26. For just as the body apart from the spirit is dead, so also faith apart from works is dead.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

This chapter divides into two great movements: the sin of partiality (vv. 1-13) and the inseparability of faith and works (vv. 14-26). The three translators were in remarkably strong agreement across most of the chapter. Where they diverged, the disagreements were largely stylistic rather than theological.

**Key consensus decisions:**

1. **"Assembly" (v. 2):** All three translators rendered synagogen as "assembly" (EMET, LOGOS) or "gathering" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA adopts "assembly," which preserves the distinctive lexical choice of James -- the only New Testament author to use synagoge for a Christian meeting -- while remaining clear in English.

2. **"The Lord of glory" (v. 1):** The Sinaiticus genitive chain tes doxes is rendered in apposition -- "the Lord of glory" -- following LOGOS, which captures the Greek construction more faithfully than PNEUMA's "our glorious Lord" (an adjectival rendering) while being clearer than EMET's hyper-literal "of the glory."

3. **"Idle" (v. 20):** All three translators recognized the distinctive Sinaiticus reading arge ("idle, useless") over the Byzantine nekra ("dead"). SOPHIA preserves "idle" as it retains the Greek wordplay: a-erge (without-work) describing faith without erga (works).

4. **"Harlot" (v. 25):** EMET and LOGOS both use "harlot" for porne; PNEUMA uses "prostitute." SOPHIA adopts "harlot" as the more traditional biblical register, and because both majority translators agreed.

5. **"Triumphs over" (v. 13):** EMET rendered katakauchatai as "boasts over," LOGOS as "triumphs over," PNEUMA as "triumphs over." SOPHIA follows the 2-to-1 consensus with "triumphs over," which conveys the force of mercy's victory more clearly in English.

6. **"The faith" (v. 14):** All three translators preserved the article before pistis in some form. SOPHIA retains "the faith" to mark James's pointed reference to that specific, workless faith -- not faith in general.

7. **"Empty man" (v. 20):** EMET and LOGOS render kene anthrope as "O empty man"; PNEUMA renders "you foolish person." SOPHIA follows the majority with "O empty man," which is closer to the Greek kenos (empty, vain) and preserves the vocative address.

8. **"Spirit" vs. "breath" (v. 26):** EMET and LOGOS render pneumatos as "spirit"; PNEUMA renders it "breath." Both are valid meanings. SOPHIA adopts "spirit" as the majority reading and the more theologically resonant term in this analogy.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Category | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Decision | Rationale |
|-------|----------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | Phrasing | "Brothers of me" | "My brothers" | "My brothers and sisters" | "My brothers" | Greek adelphoi mou is masculine plural; "my brothers" is faithful and natural. PNEUMA's inclusive "brothers and sisters" is interpretive. |
| 1 | Theology/Syntax | "the faith of the Lord of us Yeshua Christos of the glory" | "the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory" | "faith in our glorious Lord Jesus Christ" | "the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory" | LOGOS best balances fidelity to the genitive chain with clarity. PNEUMA shifts to adjectival "glorious" and objective genitive "faith in." |
| 1 | Vocabulary | "partiality" | "partiality toward persons" | "play favorites with people" | "partiality" | EMET's concise rendering captures prosopolempsiais; LOGOS expands; PNEUMA paraphrases. |
| 2 | Vocabulary | "assembly" | "assembly" | "gathering" | "assembly" | 2-to-1 agreement; preserves the synagoge distinction. |
| 2 | Phrasing | "splendid clothing" | "fine clothing" | "expensive clothes" | "fine clothing" | LOGOS balances accuracy and readability; lampra means bright/splendid/fine. |
| 3 | Phrasing | "Sit under the footstool of me" | "Sit below my footstool" | "Sit on the floor by my feet" | "Sit below my footstool" | LOGOS is faithful to hypo to hypopodion mou; PNEUMA paraphrases away the footstool image. |
| 3 | Phrasing | "You sit here well" | "You sit here in a good place" | "Here, take this excellent seat" | "You sit here in a good place" | LOGOS clarifies kalos ("well/in a good place") without PNEUMA's elaboration. |
| 4 | Vocabulary | "judges of evil reasonings" | "judges with evil reasonings" | "judges with corrupt motives" | "judges with evil reasonings" | "Evil reasonings" (dialogismon poneron) is more literal than "corrupt motives." EMET and LOGOS agree on "evil reasonings." |
| 5 | Phrasing | "beloved brothers of me" | "my beloved brothers" | "my dear brothers and sisters" | "my beloved brothers" | LOGOS is natural English; PNEUMA adds "sisters" interpretively. |
| 5 | Syntax | "the poor of the world, rich in faith" | "the poor of the world to be rich in faith" | "the people the world considers poor to be rich in faith" | "the poor of the world to be rich in faith" | LOGOS captures the infinitival purpose; PNEUMA expands "of the world" interpretively. |
| 6 | Vocabulary | "dishonored" | "dishonored" | "humiliated" | "dishonored" | 2-to-1; atimazein means to dishonor/treat shamefully. |
| 7 | Phrasing | "the good name, the one having been called upon you" | "the good name that was invoked over you" | "the noble name by which you have been called" | "the good name that was invoked over you" | kalon = "good/noble." EMET and LOGOS agree on "good"; PNEUMA uses "noble." "Invoked over you" follows the Greek epiklēthen eph' hymas. |
| 9 | Vocabulary | "show partiality" / "work sin" | "show partiality toward persons" / "commit sin" | "show favoritism" / "committing sin" | "show partiality" / "commit sin" | Blended: EMET's concise "show partiality" + LOGOS/PNEUMA's "commit sin" (more natural than "work sin"). |
| 11 | Phrasing | "the one having said" | "he who said" | "the same God who said" | "he who said" | LOGOS is clear and faithful. PNEUMA adds "God" (implied but not in Greek). |
| 12 | Vocabulary | "law of freedom" | "law of liberty" | "law that sets people free" | "law of liberty" | "Law of liberty" is the established English rendering; PNEUMA paraphrases. |
| 13 | Vocabulary | "merciless" / "boasts over" | "without mercy" / "triumphs over" | "merciless" / "triumphs over" | "without mercy" / "triumphs over" | "Without mercy" (LOGOS) for aneleos is precise; "triumphs over" has 2-to-1 support. |
| 14 | Phrasing | "if someone should say he has faith, but should not have works" | "if someone says he has faith but does not have works" | "if someone claims to have faith but has nothing to show for it" | "if someone says he has faith but does not have works" | LOGOS is clear and faithful. PNEUMA's "nothing to show for it" is interpretive. |
| 15 | Phrasing | "lacking of the daily food" | "lacking daily food" | "nothing to eat today" | "lacking daily food" | LOGOS is concise; PNEUMA paraphrases ephemerou trophes. |
| 17 | Phrasing | "the faith, if it should not have works, is dead by itself" | "faith, if it does not have works, is dead by itself" | "faith that stands alone -- without any action to back it up -- is dead" | "faith, if it does not have works, is dead by itself" | EMET and LOGOS agree closely; PNEUMA heavily paraphrases. |
| 18 | Vocabulary | "the faith of you apart from the works" / "the works of me the faith" | "your faith apart from the works" / "faith from my works" | "your faith without any actions" / "my faith by what I do" | "your faith apart from the works" / "faith from my works" | LOGOS follows the Greek choris ton ergon precisely. PNEUMA shifts "works" to "actions" throughout. |
| 19 | Punctuation | "you do well. Even the demons believe and shudder." | "You do well; the demons also believe, and shudder." | "Good for you! Even the demons believe that -- and they shudder in terror." | "You do well; even the demons believe, and shudder." | Blended punctuation from LOGOS with "even" from EMET/PNEUMA. PNEUMA adds "in terror" (not in Greek). |
| 20 | Vocabulary | "O empty man" | "O empty man" | "you foolish person" | "O empty man" | 2-to-1; kenos means "empty/vain," not "foolish" (which would be moros). |
| 20 | Vocabulary | "idle" | "idle" | "useless" | "idle" | 2-to-1; arge = "idle" preserving the a-erge/erga wordplay. |
| 21 | Names | "Abraam" / "Isaak" | "Abraham" / "Isaac" | "Abraham" / "Isaac" | "Abraham" / "Isaac" | 2-to-1; anglicized forms are standard. EMET's transliterations are noted. |
| 21 | Phrasing | "was he not justified from works, having offered up Isaak" | "was not Abraham our father justified by works when he offered up Isaac" | "wasn't our ancestor Abraham shown to be right with God by what he did when he offered his son Isaac" | "was not Abraham our father justified by works, when he offered up Isaac his son upon the altar" | LOGOS base with EMET's "upon the altar" included (present in all three). PNEUMA's "shown to be right with God" paraphrases dikaioo. |
| 22 | Phrasing | "the faith was working together with the works of him" | "faith was working together with his works" | "his faith and his actions were working hand in hand" | "faith was working together with his works" | EMET and LOGOS agree closely. PNEUMA paraphrases synergei as "hand in hand." |
| 23 | Vocabulary | "reckoned to him unto righteousness" | "reckoned to him as righteousness" | "credited to him as righteousness" | "reckoned to him as righteousness" | EMET and LOGOS agree on "reckoned" (logizesthai). PNEUMA uses "credited" (modern financial metaphor). |
| 24 | Phrasing | "from works a man is justified and not from faith alone" | "a man is justified by works, and not by faith alone" | "a person is made right with God by what they do, and not by faith alone" | "a man is justified by works, and not by faith alone" | EMET and LOGOS agree. PNEUMA paraphrases with gender-neutral "person" and "made right with God." |
| 25 | Vocabulary | "Raab the harlot" | "Rahab the harlot" | "Rahab the prostitute" | "Rahab the harlot" | 2-to-1 on "harlot." EMET's "Raab" is transliterated but non-standard in English. |
| 25 | Vocabulary | "messengers" | "messengers" | "spies" | "messengers" | 2-to-1; aggelous = "messengers." PNEUMA's "spies" interprets their role (cf. Joshua 2). |
| 26 | Vocabulary | "spirit" | "spirit" | "breath" | "spirit" | 2-to-1; pneumatos can mean either, but "spirit" is traditional and matches the body/spirit theological pairing. |
| General | Register | Hebraic names (Yeshua Christos, Abraam, Isaak, Raab) | Standard anglicized names | Standard anglicized names | Standard anglicized names | 2-to-1; anglicized forms are conventional in English Bibles. |
| General | Vocabulary | "works" throughout | "works" throughout | "actions" or "what he/she did" throughout | "works" throughout | 2-to-1; erga is traditionally rendered "works" in theological discourse; PNEUMA's "actions" loses the faith-works theological framework. |
